comment_text:
Jab Hook believes that "Canelo" will not be able to keep up with Crawford's movement. Alvarez will struggle to hit Omaha's hero cleanly and will suffer the nasty retribution of counter-punching combinations. The early rounds will roll by as Crawford outworks "Canelo" and repeatedly takes the 10 points, which assumes "Bud" can handle "Canelo's" power punches. 

"Canelo" will try to fight back, but the scorecards will force him into knockout tactics in the later rounds, as he hopes to save himself with a left hook to the “Bud's” liver. Lefties are kryptonite for Saul Alvarez: the great Mayweather embarrassed Alvarez, Erislandy Lara boxed him to a split decision. “Bud” Crawford will follow this southpaw suit. 

Jab Hook predicts an excellent, exciting bout with some amazing exchanges of punches. Some rounds will be tough to score, but the general trend of the fight will be Crawford's greater offensive output and his ability to avoid getting hit by Canelo's occasional combinations. 

Cash-cow boxing icons always get the benefit of the doubt from established boxing judges, especially in Las Vegas. Therefore, the scorecards will be closer than the fight itself. ”...and new, unified world super middleweight champion, “Bud” Crawford by split decision.”

video_title: Canelo vs. Crawford | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Fair ⚠️warning ⚠️ this is a deep dive. But if you’re into betting and intrigued by this fight, stick with me.

Canelo is being unfairly judged based on his last performance. Let’s be real: that fight was against an opponent who didn’t bring much out of him. Motivation matters, and things will look very different when he’s standing across from Crawford.

Now, let’s talk size. On paper, Crawford has the longer reach and taller frame but there’s a difference between physical dimensions and actual fighting weight. Crawford is essentially jumping up three weight classes. Yes, he fought at 154 once, but he’s never had a single fight at 160, let alone 168. No tune up, no feel out fights plus, he’s been inactive.

Even if you believe Canelo has lost a step, you have to consider this: he’s taken shots from true light heavyweights. His chin is proven. I just don’t see a scenario where Crawford, coming up in weight, has the power to stop him. It’s not realistic, especially not via TKO or KO.

Lastly, I keep hearing comparisons to Mayweather. Let me clear this up Crawford is not Floyd. He doesn’t fight like Floyd, never has, never will. Crawford gets hit. He’s not a pure defensive genius; he’s a fighter who likes to exchange. Sure, he might give you a round or two of slick movement, but he’s not that guy for 12 rounds. I just don’t see it.
